subject: Chemistry
q: When two mole of an ideal gas ($C_{p,m} = \frac{5}{2}R$) heated from 300 K to 600 K at constant pressure. The change in entropy of gas ($\Delta S$) is :
question: When two mole of an ideal gas ($C_{p,m} = \frac{5}{2}R$) heated from 300 K to 600 K at constant pressure. The change in entropy of gas ($\Delta S$) is :
solution:
For an ideal gas heated at constant pressure, the entropy change is

$$ \Delta S = nC_{p,m}\ln \frac{T_2}{T_1} $$

Here,

$$ n = 2 $$

$$ C_{p,m} = \frac{5}{2}R $$

$$ T_1 = 300\,K,\quad T_2 = 600\,K $$

So,

$$ \Delta S = 2 \times \frac{5}{2}R \ln \frac{600}{300} $$

$$ \Delta S = 5R \ln 2 $$

Hence, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{5R\ln 2} $$

Option C.

subject: Chemistry
question: Entropy of universe decreases during
options: vaporization of camphor above normal sublimation temperature and 1 atm.
solution:
The entropy of the universe decreases during a non-spontaneous process.

For any process,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}=\Delta S_{\text{system}}+\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} $$

For a spontaneous process,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}>0 $$

For a reversible process at equilibrium,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}=0 $$

For a non-spontaneous process,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}<0 $$

Now check the options.

In an unsaturated solution of sucrose, more sucrose can still dissolve. So crystallization of sucrose from an unsaturated solution will not happen spontaneously. It is a non-spontaneous process.

So for this process,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}<0 $$

Rusting of iron is spontaneous, so entropy of universe increases.

Melting of ice at $0^\circ \text{C}$ and $1$ atm is an equilibrium process, so entropy of universe remains unchanged.

Vaporization of camphor above its normal sublimation temperature is spontaneous, so entropy of universe increases.

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A: crystallization of sucrose from unsaturated solution}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: If one mole of an ideal gas ( $C_{p,m} = \frac{5}{2}R$ ) is expanded isothermally at 300 K until its volume is tripled, then change in entropy of gas is:
question: If one mole of an ideal gas ( $C_{p,m} = \frac{5}{2}R$ ) is expanded isothermally at 300 K until its volume is tripled, then change in entropy of gas is:
solution:
For an ideal gas, the entropy change during an isothermal expansion is given by

$$ \Delta S = nR \ln \frac{V_2}{V_1} $$

Here, one mole of gas is taken, so

$$ n = 1 $$

The volume is tripled, so

$$ V_2 = 3V_1 $$

Substituting these values,

$$ \Delta S = 1 \times R \ln \frac{3V_1}{V_1} $$

$$ \Delta S = R \ln 3 $$

The given value of $C_{p,m} = \frac{5}{2}R$ is not needed here because the process is isothermal. For an ideal gas in an isothermal process, entropy change depends only on the volume ratio.

So, the change in entropy of the gas is

$$ \boxed{R \ln 3} $$

Correct option: D.

subject: Chemistry
q: When one mole of an ideal gas is compressed to half of its initial volume and simultaneously heated to twice its temperature, the change in entropy is
question: When one mole of an ideal gas is compressed to half of its initial volume and simultaneously heated to twice its temperature, the change in entropy is
solution:
For one mole of an ideal gas, entropy change is given by

$$ \Delta S = C_{V,m}\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + R\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1} $$

Here, the gas is heated to twice its initial temperature, so

$$ \frac{T_2}{T_1}=2 $$

It is compressed to half of its initial volume, so

$$ \frac{V_2}{V_1}=\frac{1}{2} $$

Substitute these values:

$$ \Delta S = C_{V,m}\ln 2 + R\ln\frac{1}{2} $$

Since

$$ \ln\frac{1}{2}=-\ln 2 $$

we get

$$ \Delta S = C_{V,m}\ln 2 - R\ln 2 $$

$$ \Delta S = (C_{V,m}-R)\ln 2 $$

So the correct option is

$$ \boxed{(C_{V,m}-R)\ln 2} $$

Option D.

subject: Chemistry
q: The entropy change in the fusion of one mole of a solid melting at 300 K (latent heat of fusion, 2930 J/mol) is
question: The entropy change in the fusion of one mole of a solid melting at 300 K (latent heat of fusion, 2930 J/mol) is
solution:
For fusion at the melting point, the entropy change is given by

$$ \Delta S_{\text{fusion}}=\frac{\Delta H_{\text{fusion}}}{T} $$

Here,

$$ \Delta H_{\text{fusion}} = 2930 \ \text{J mol}^{-1} $$

and

$$ T = 300 \ \text{K} $$

So,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{fusion}}=\frac{2930}{300} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{fusion}}=9.77 \ \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{9.77 \ \text{J/K-mol}} $$

Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The standard molar entropy of an ideal gas
$\gamma = \frac{4}{3}$ is 2.5 cal/K-mol at 25°C and 1 bar. The standard molar entropy of the gas at 323°C and 1 bar is (ln 2 = 0.7)
question:
The standard molar entropy of an ideal gas
$\gamma = \frac{4}{3}$ is 2.5 cal/K-mol at 25°C and 1 bar. The standard molar entropy of the gas at 323°C and 1 bar is (ln 2 = 0.7)
solution:
For an ideal gas at constant pressure, the change in molar entropy is

$$\Delta S = C_p \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

Here $\gamma = 4/3$, so

$$C_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{(4/3)R}{1/3} = 4R$$

Temperatures:

$T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298$ K

$T_2 = 323 + 273 = 596$ K

So $T_2/T_1 = 2$, and

$$\Delta S = 4R \ln 2$$

Taking $R = 2$ cal/K-mol and $\ln 2 = 0.7$:

$$\Delta S = 4 \times 2 \times 0.7 = 5.6 \text{ cal/K-mol}$$

Now the final entropy is

$$S_2 = S_1 + \Delta S = 2.5 + 5.6 = 8.1 \text{ cal/K-mol}$$

So the correct answer is option D, $8.1$ cal/K-mol.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given the following entropy values (in J/K-mol) at 298 K and 1 atm:

H2(g) = 130.6, Cl2(g) = 223.0 and HCl(g) = 186.7.

The entropy change (in J/K-mol) for the reaction

H2(g) + Cl2(g) → 2HCl(g) is
question:
Given the following entropy values (in J/K-mol) at 298 K and 1 atm:

H2(g) = 130.6, Cl2(g) = 223.0 and HCl(g) = 186.7.

The entropy change (in J/K-mol) for the reaction

H2(g) + Cl2(g) → 2HCl(g) is
solution:
For the reaction

$$ \text{H}_2(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{HCl}(g) $$

Entropy change is found using

$$ \Delta S = \sum S_{\text{products}} - \sum S_{\text{reactants}} $$

Here, the product is $2\text{HCl}(g)$, so its entropy contribution is

$$ 2 \times 186.7 = 373.4 \ \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

The reactants are $\text{H}_2(g)$ and $\text{Cl}_2(g)$, so their total entropy is

$$ 130.6 + 223.0 = 353.6 \ \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

Now,

$$ \Delta S = 373.4 - 353.6 $$

$$ \Delta S = +19.8 \ \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

So, the entropy change is

$$ \boxed{+19.8 \ \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1}} $$

Correct option: D.

subject: Chemistry
q: For which of the following process, $\Delta S$ is negative?
question: For which of the following process, $\Delta S$ is negative?
solution:
For a process, $\Delta S$ is negative when randomness or disorder decreases.

Check each option:

A.

$$ \text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{H}(g) $$

One gaseous molecule breaks into two gaseous atoms. Number of particles increases, so randomness increases.

So, $\Delta S$ is positive.

B.

$$ \text{N}_2(g, 1\ \text{atm}) \rightarrow \text{N}_2(g, 8\ \text{atm}) $$

Pressure increases from $1$ atm to $8$ atm. This means the gas is compressed, so its volume decreases.

For a gas,

$$ \Delta S = nR \ln \frac{V_2}{V_1} $$

Since $V_2 < V_1$,

$$ \ln \frac{V_2}{V_1} < 0 $$

So,

$$ \Delta S < 0 $$

C.

$$ 2\text{SO}_3(g) \rightarrow 2\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) $$

Here, $2$ moles of gaseous reactants form $3$ moles of gaseous products. More gas molecules means more randomness.

So, $\Delta S$ is positive.

D.

$$ \text{C (Diamond)} \rightarrow \text{C (Graphite)} $$

Graphite has more entropy than diamond, so this change has positive $\Delta S$.

Hence, the process for which $\Delta S$ is negative is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option B}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: For which reaction from the following, will be maximum entropy change :
question: For which reaction from the following, will be maximum entropy change :
solution:
The maximum entropy change will be for:

$$\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightarrow \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$$

So, the correct option is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option B}}$$

Entropy is a measure of randomness or disorder.

In option B, one solid substance decomposes to give another solid and a gas:

$$\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightarrow \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$$

A gas has much more randomness than a solid. Since a gaseous product is formed from a solid reactant, the entropy increases greatly.

Checking the other options:

Option A:

$$\text{Ca}(s) + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CaO}(s)$$

Here gas is consumed and solid is formed, so entropy decreases.

Option C:

$$\text{C}(s) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g)$$

There is one mole of gas on both sides, so entropy change is small.

Option D:

$$\text{N}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{NO}(g)$$

There are 2 moles of gas on both sides, so entropy change is not very large.

Hence, the maximum increase in entropy occurs in:

$$\boxed{\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightarrow \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The entropy change accompanying the transfer of 12,000 J of heat from a body A at 327°C to a body B at 127°C is
question: The entropy change accompanying the transfer of 12,000 J of heat from a body A at 327°C to a body B at 127°C is
solution:
Temperature of body A:

$$ 327^\circ \text{C} = 327 + 273 = 600 \text{ K} $$

Temperature of body B:

$$ 127^\circ \text{C} = 127 + 273 = 400 \text{ K} $$

Heat is transferred from body A to body B.

So, body A loses heat:

$$ \Delta S_A = \frac{-Q}{T_A} $$

Body B gains heat:

$$ \Delta S_B = \frac{+Q}{T_B} $$

Total entropy change is

$$ \Delta S = \Delta S_A + \Delta S_B $$

$$ \Delta S = \frac{-12000}{600} + \frac{12000}{400} $$

$$ \Delta S = -20 + 30 $$

$$ \Delta S = +10 \text{ J/K} $$

So, the entropy change is

$$ \boxed{+10.0 \text{ J/K}} $$

Correct option: Option B.

subject: Chemistry
q: Two mole of an ideal gas is expanded irreversibly and isothermally at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ until its volume is doubled and 3.41 kJ heat is absorbed from surrounding. $\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {iotal }}$ (system + surrounding) in $\mathrm{J} / \mathrm{K}$ is : (Report your answer answer multiplying with 100)
question: Two mole of an ideal gas is expanded irreversibly and isothermally at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ until its volume is doubled and 3.41 kJ heat is absorbed from surrounding. $\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {iotal }}$ (system + surrounding) in $\mathrm{J} / \mathrm{K}$ is : (Report your answer answer multiplying with 100)
solution:
For an ideal gas undergoing isothermal expansion, entropy change of the system depends only on initial and final states.

Here,

$$ n=2,\quad T=37^\circ \text{C}=310\text{ K},\quad \frac{V_2}{V_1}=2 $$

Entropy change of the system is

$$ \Delta S_{\text{system}}=nR\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{system}}=2\times 8.314 \times \ln 2 $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{system}}=2\times 8.314 \times 0.693 $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{system}}\approx 11.52\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

The gas absorbs heat, so

$$ q_{\text{system}}=+3.41\ \text{kJ} $$

Hence the surroundings lose the same amount of heat:

$$ q_{\text{surrounding}}=-3.41\ \text{kJ}=-3410\ \text{J} $$

Entropy change of surroundings is

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surrounding}}=\frac{q_{\text{surrounding}}}{T} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surrounding}}=\frac{-3410}{310} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surrounding}}=-11.00\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

So total entropy change is

$$ \Delta S_{\text{total}}=\Delta S_{\text{system}}+\Delta S_{\text{surrounding}} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{total}}=11.52-11.00 $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{total}}\approx 0.52\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

The question asks to report the answer after multiplying by $100$:

$$ 0.52\times 100 \approx 52 $$

So the answer is

$$ \boxed{52} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
How many of the following reactions has a positive entropy change?

I. $\quad \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})$

II. $\quad \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_3(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})$

III. $2 \mathrm{NH}_3(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})$

IV. $\quad 2 \mathrm{SO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_3(\mathrm{~g})$

V. $\quad \mathrm{PCl}_5(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{PCl}_3(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
question:
How many of the following reactions has a positive entropy change?

I. $\quad \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})$

II. $\quad \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_3(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})$

III. $2 \mathrm{NH}_3(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})$

IV. $\quad 2 \mathrm{SO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_3(\mathrm{~g})$

V. $\quad \mathrm{PCl}_5(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{PCl}_3(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
solution:
Entropy increases when disorder increases. In general:

  • Formation of gases from solids or liquids gives positive entropy change.

  • Increase in number of gaseous moles gives positive entropy change.

  • Formation of a solid from aqueous ions usually gives negative entropy change.

Now check each reaction.

I.

$$ \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(aq)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(aq)\longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(s) $$

Two aqueous ions combine to form a solid precipitate. The particles become more ordered.

So, entropy change is negative.

II.

$$ \mathrm{NH_4Cl}(s)\longrightarrow \mathrm{NH_3}(g)+\mathrm{HCl}(g) $$

One solid forms two gases. Gases have much more randomness than solids.

So, entropy change is positive.

III.

$$ 2\mathrm{NH_3}(g)\longrightarrow \mathrm{N_2}(g)+3\mathrm{H_2}(g) $$

Number of gaseous moles changes from $2$ to $4$.

So, entropy change is positive.

IV.

$$ 2\mathrm{SO_2}(g)+\mathrm{O_2}(g)\longrightarrow 2\mathrm{SO_3}(g) $$

Number of gaseous moles changes from $3$ to $2$.

So, entropy change is negative.

V.

$$ \mathrm{PCl_5}(g)\longrightarrow \mathrm{PCl_3}(g)+\mathrm{Cl_2}(g) $$

Number of gaseous moles changes from $1$ to $2$.

So, entropy change is positive.

Hence, the reactions with positive entropy change are II, III and V.

$$ \boxed{3} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: 5.85 g of NaCl on melting absorbed 3.05 kJ of heat and its entropy is increased by $28.8 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Thus, melting point of sodium chloride is (in K) :
question: 5.85 g of NaCl on melting absorbed 3.05 kJ of heat and its entropy is increased by $28.8 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Thus, melting point of sodium chloride is (in K) :
solution:
For melting at the melting point, the entropy change is related to heat of fusion by

$$ \Delta S_{\text{fus}}=\frac{\Delta H_{\text{fus}}}{T_m} $$

Given mass of NaCl:

$$ 5.85 \text{ g} $$

Molar mass of NaCl:

$$ 58.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

So, number of moles of NaCl:

$$ n=\frac{5.85}{58.5}=0.1 \text{ mol} $$

Heat absorbed by $0.1$ mol NaCl:

$$ q=3.05 \text{ kJ} $$

So, heat absorbed by $1$ mol NaCl:

$$ \Delta H_{\text{fus}}=\frac{3.05}{0.1}=30.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} $$

Convert it into joules:

$$ \Delta H_{\text{fus}}=30.5 \times 1000=30500 \text{ J mol}^{-1} $$

Given entropy change:

$$ \Delta S_{\text{fus}}=28.8 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1} $$

Now,

$$ T_m=\frac{\Delta H_{\text{fus}}}{\Delta S_{\text{fus}}} $$

$$ T_m=\frac{30500}{28.8} $$

$$ T_m \approx 1059 \text{ K} $$

So, the melting point of sodium chloride is

$$ \boxed{1059 \text{ K}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A quantity of 0.5 mole of an ideal gas at 300 K expands isothermally against a constant pressure of 2.0 atm from 1.0 L to 5.0 L .

Thus, $(\Delta S)_{\text {universe }}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{JK}^{-1}$.
question:
A quantity of 0.5 mole of an ideal gas at 300 K expands isothermally against a constant pressure of 2.0 atm from 1.0 L to 5.0 L .

Thus, $(\Delta S)_{\text {universe }}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{JK}^{-1}$.
solution:
For an ideal gas expanding isothermally, the entropy change of the gas is

$$ \Delta S_{\text{system}}=nR\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1} $$

Here,

$$ n=0.5\ \text{mol},\quad T=300\ \text{K},\quad V_1=1.0\ \text{L},\quad V_2=5.0\ \text{L} $$

So,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{system}}=0.5 \times 8.314 \times \ln 5 $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{system}}=6.69\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

Since the expansion is against a constant external pressure,

$$ w=-P_{\text{ext}}\Delta V $$

$$ w=-2.0 \times (5.0-1.0) $$

$$ w=-8.0\ \text{L atm} $$

Converting into joules,

$$ w=-8.0 \times 101.3=-810.4\ \text{J} $$

For an isothermal process of an ideal gas,

$$ \Delta U=0 $$

So,

$$ q=-w=810.4\ \text{J} $$

This heat is absorbed by the gas, so the surroundings lose the same amount of heat:

$$ q_{\text{surroundings}}=-810.4\ \text{J} $$

Thus,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}=\frac{q_{\text{surroundings}}}{T} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}=\frac{-810.4}{300} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}=-2.70\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

Now,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}=\Delta S_{\text{system}}+\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}=6.69-2.70 $$

$$ \Delta S_{\text{universe}}=3.99\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

So,

$$ (\Delta S)_{\text{universe}} \approx 4.0\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: An ideal gas undergoes irreversible isothermal expansion at 298 K and performs 1000 J of work. Its entropy change is $10 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$. Hence, the ratio of $W_{\text {rev }}$ to $W_{\text {irrev }}$ is (In nearest integer)
question: An ideal gas undergoes irreversible isothermal expansion at 298 K and performs 1000 J of work. Its entropy change is $10 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$. Hence, the ratio of $W_{\text {rev }}$ to $W_{\text {irrev }}$ is (In nearest integer)
solution:
For an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, the entropy change is given by

$$ \Delta S=\frac{q_{\text{rev}}}{T} $$

Given,

$$ \Delta S = 10\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

and

$$ T = 298\ \text{K} $$

So,

$$ q_{\text{rev}} = T\Delta S $$

$$ q_{\text{rev}} = 298 \times 10 = 2980\ \text{J} $$

For an ideal gas in isothermal expansion,

$$ \Delta U = 0 $$

So heat absorbed is equal to work done in magnitude. Hence,

$$ W_{\text{rev}} = 2980\ \text{J} $$

Given irreversible work is

$$ W_{\text{irrev}} = 1000\ \text{J} $$

Thus,

$$ \frac{W_{\text{rev}}}{W_{\text{irrev}}} = \frac{2980}{1000} = 2.98 $$

Nearest integer:

$$ \boxed{3} $$
